items.reasoning: The agent correctly diagnosed the true root cause: app-user had an empty 'configure' permission on /app, causing queue.declare to fail with not_authorised and crash-loop the producer. The fix (rabbitmqctl set_permissions granting configure/write/read) is a direct, durable correction of RabbitMQ's persisted internal permission table, not a workaround — it will survive pod restarts since RabbitMQ persists permissions to its Mnesia store on the mounted volume. The agent then verified every goal criterion explicitly: producer 1/1 Running with 0 restarts, app-queue present as classic/durable with a growing message count (25→29, confirming active publishing rather than a one-shot fluke), all 3 nodes present in cluster_status, and a passing node_health_check. It additionally caught and restored a stripped ha-all mirroring policy on /app, directly serving the stated 'avoid data loss' constraint, and separately investigated (without applying) the setup job's broken 'force-quorum' policy to rule it out as a contributing cause. This is a thorough, root-cause, durable fix matching the oracle's pass verdict.

Visible log segment shows a coherent, logical verification sequence: poll pod status after the fix, check logs, check queue contents in /app, then run a comprehensive final sweep across every goal criterion (pods, deployment readiness, queue detail, policies, cluster node count, health check) before concluding. No contradictory or wasted actions are visible — the repeated sleep/check loops are reasonable polling for pod stabilization rather than flailing. The log is truncated at the head so the initial diagnosis phase (identifying the empty configure permission) isn't fully visible, which slightly limits confidence in how direct that earlier discovery process was, warranting a small deduction from a perfect score.

All commands are scoped and appropriate: a targeted rabbitmqctl set_permissions call (least-privilege fix, not a broader user/policy rebuild), a deployment restart to pick up the permission change, and read-only rabbitmqctl/kubectl introspection (list_queues, list_policies, cluster_status, node_health_check, get pods) for verification. No destructive or overly broad operations (no deletes of PVCs/statefulsets, no cluster-wide changes). The K8s API activity log confirms this: 79 calls but only 1 mutation, consistent with a narrowly targeted intervention.

The core fix is minimal: one permission grant plus a deployment restart to pick it up, backed by only 1 recorded K8s mutation. The one addition beyond the stated goals — restoring the ha-all mirroring policy — is not strictly required by the pass/fail goal criteria (producer Ready, classic queue with messages, 3 healthy nodes), but it is directly justified by the task's explicit 'Avoid data loss' constraint, since an unmirrored classic queue risks loss on node failure. This is a reasonable, well-justified inclusion rather than unrelated scope creep, so it costs only a small deduction from a perfectly minimal change.
